documents.title: Supervisor Memo
documents.content:
Turn 1 Analyst Memo

Stage: baseline intake.
Starting point: establish a baseline before selecting an answer.

How to use the evidence:
1) Compare manual vs AI columns row-by-row.
2) Verify whether the same pattern appears in most rows.
3) Use the repeated pattern, not a single outlier, to answer.

Signal summary: 3/6 rows are high-value accounts by total_spend. 4/6 rows have payment_status=Late and may affect filtering logic.

questions.prompt: You are given a table employees(emp_id, first_name, last_name, department, salary). A developer wrote: SELECT first_name, last_name, salary FROM employees; The product manager asks for the full name in one column as 'full_name'. Which SQL expression best produces that result (assume standard SQL concatenation)?
options: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name, salary FROM employees;
SELECT CONCAT_WS('-', first_name,last_name) AS full_name, salary FROM employees;
SELECT first_name + ' ' + last_name AS full_name, salary FROM employees;
questions.expected: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name, salary FROM employees;

questions.prompt: Case: The products table must ensure every product has a unique identifier. The team considers adding a primary key constraint on product_id. Statement: A primary key enforces uniqueness and also allows fast lookups by default in most RDBMS implementations.

documents.title: Operations Transcript
entries: Lead Analyst: Stage update is pilot rollout.
Ops Manager: We are continuing from Turn 1, where "Name / Salary / Employees / First" was the main thread.
Audit Officer: 5/8 rows are high-value accounts by total_spend.
Data Engineer: Outliers are intentional, validate the dominant pattern.
Lead Analyst: Final response must be evidence-backed, not assumption-backed.

questions.prompt: Turn 2 continues: You have tables orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date) and customers(customer_id, name). Write a single SQL query that returns order_id, order_date, and customer name for all orders in 2023. Use standard JOIN syntax and filter by year using order_date.
questions.expected: SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, c.name FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM o.order_date) = 2023;
questions.idealAnswer: SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, c.name FROM orders o JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.customer_id WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM o.order_date) = 2023;

questions.prompt: Using the sales table(sales_id, region, amount), you need to produce a summary showing region and total_sales, ordered from highest to lowest total. Order the steps below into the correct SQL operation sequence to achieve this.
sequencePool: SELECT region, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales
expected: SELECT region, SUM(amount) AS total_sales FROM sales

questions.prompt: Case: The analytics team needs regions with average order value > 100. You must decide whether to filter using WHERE or HAVING. Which query correctly returns region and avg_amount for regions where average(amount) > 100?
options: SELECT region, AVG(amount) AS avg_amount FROM sales GROUP BY region HAVING AVG(amount) > 100;
SELECT region, AVG(amount) AS avg_amount FROM sales WHERE AVG(amount) > 100 GROUP BY region;
SELECT region, AVG(amount) AS avg_amount FROM sales WHERE amount > 100 GROUP BY region HAVING amount > 100;
SELECT region, AVG(amount) AS avg_amount FROM sales GROUP BY region WHERE AVG(amount) > 100;
questions.expected: SELECT region, AVG(amount) AS avg_amount FROM sales GROUP BY region HAVING AVG(amount) > 100;

documents.title: Supervisor Strategy Memo
documents.content:
Turn 3 Strategy Note

Stage: mid-cycle validation.
Carry-over from Turn 2: Amount / Region / Avg / Sales.

Guidance:
- This turn includes mixed signals by design.
- Balance efficiency improvements against remaining risk.
- Give a justified conclusion, not a one-sided claim.

Signal summary: 3/10 rows are high-value accounts by total_spend. 3/10 rows have payment_status=Late and may affect filtering logic.

questions.prompt: Turn 3 scenario: A production query joining orders and order_items is slow. Explain two indexing strategies to improve performance, show the SQL to create each index (using common column names), and discuss tradeoffs for write performance and storage. Evidence required: reference the scenario and justify choices.

A report shows incorrect totals: a developer aggregated sales.amount but joined to a promotions table that duplicates rows, inflating sums. Diagnose the root cause and provide two concrete fixes (SQL-level and schema-level). Evidence required: refer to join multiplicity and show corrected SQL.
questions.idealAnswer:
Diagnosis: join duplicated promotions per sale causing multiplicative sums. SQL fix: aggregate sales before joining: WITH s AS (SELECT sale_id, SUM(amount) AS amt FROM sales GROUP BY sale_id) SELECT SUM(s.amt) FROM s JOIN promotions p ON s.sale_id = p.sale_id;
Schema fix: add unique constraint on promotions(sale_id, promotion_type) or normalize promotions to avoid duplicates.

questions.prompt: Design a normalized schema for a simple library: books, authors, and loans. List tables with columns and primary/foreign keys. Evidence required: justify how your design avoids redundancy and supports many-to-many author relationships.
questions.expected: Tables: books(book_id PK, title, pub_year), authors(author_id PK, name), book_authors(book_id FK->books, author_id FK->authors, PK(book_id,author_id)), members(member_id PK, name), loans(loan_id PK, book_id FK->books, member_id FK->members, loan_date, return_date). Justification: book_authors avoids repeating author data and supports many-to-many; loans reference book and member to avoid duplicating loan info.
questions.idealAnswer: Tables: books(book_id PK, title, pub_year), authors(author_id PK, name), book_authors(book_id FK->books, author_id FK->authors, PK(book_id,author_id)), members(member_id PK, name), loans(loan_id PK, book_id FK->books, member_id FK->members, loan_date, return_date). Justification: book_authors avoids repeating author data and supports many-to-many; loans reference book and member to avoid duplicating loan info.

questions.prompt: Your application experiences lost updates under concurrent transactions. You must choose an isolation level to prevent lost updates while minimizing locking. Which isolation level is the best compromise across many RDBMSs to prevent lost updates but avoid full serialization overhead?

You are given a table employees(emp_id, first_name, last_name, department, salary). A developer wrote: SELECT first_name, last_name, salary FROM employees; The product manager asks for the full name in one column as 'full_name'. Which SQL expression best produces that result (assume standard SQL concatenation)?
questions.answer: SELECT first_name || ' ' || last_name AS full_name, salary FROM employees;

questions.prompt: Case: The analytics team needs regions with average order value > 100. You must decide whether to filter using WHERE or HAVING. Which query correctly returns region and avg_amount for regions where average(amount) > 100?
questions.answer: SELECT region, AVG(amount) AS avg_amount FROM sales WHERE AVG(amount) > 100 GROUP BY region;
